documents.title: Regulation for the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence Systems with Institutional Data

ai_strategy.summary: ECU has a central AI@ECU hub with principles for data protection, academic integrity, transparency, accessibility, AI literacy, responsible innovation, and research integrity. Its 2025 regulation governs generative AI use with institutional data and is complemented by library, teaching, student, and business-center resources.

key_provisions: Use AI in accordance with academic integrity expectations and instructor or unit guidance
Do not enter restricted, confidential, regulated, or sensitive data into unapproved AI tools
Review AI outputs for accuracy, hallucination, bias, copyright, and attribution issues
Disclose or cite AI use when required by course, research, publisher, or unit rules
Keep human judgment and accountability in consequential academic, research, and administrative decisions
ai_policy.summary: East Carolina University's AI policy posture is best characterized as comprehensive: public sources show explicit expectations for responsible use, privacy, disclosure, academic integrity, and human review, while leaving some implementation decisions to units or instructors.

initiatives.title: Microsoft Copilot approved for protected institutional data
initiatives.description: ECU identifies Microsoft Copilot as the primary approved generative AI tool for Level 2 protected institutional data through the Microsoft 365 subscription and single sign-on.
initiatives.significance: This closes a platform gap with named approved enterprise tool and data-classification guardrails.

regulatory_context.summary: East Carolina University operates in North Carolina; public AI guidance should be read alongside applicable state, federal, and institutional privacy, security, accessibility, and academic-integrity requirements.

narrative_summary: East Carolina University (Greenville, NC) is an R1 institution whose public AI posture shows activity across governance, teaching, research, enterprise tooling, workforce readiness, and access. ECU has a central AI@ECU hub with principles for data protection, academic integrity, transparency, accessibility, AI literacy, responsible innovation, and research integrity. Its 2025 regulation governs generative AI use with institutional data and is complemented by library, teaching, student, and business-center resources. The most concrete public initiatives captured here are AI@ECU central hub; Generative AI regulation for institutional data; Center for AI in Business; Microsoft Copilot access for students; Industry AI Perspectives Conference. Across the profile, the strongest evidence comes from official university AI hubs, policy pages, teaching resources, research-center pages, and program pages rather than secondary reporting. The institution_type is set to r1_research from the canonical Carnegie R1 list. Known gaps are intentionally explicit: leadership names, user counts, detailed budgets, and compute counts are only included when they surfaced in public sources. Overall, the profile supports at least five AI initiatives and evidences strategy/leadership, governance, teaching/curriculum, research infrastructure, student services or enterprise tooling, workforce/community, partnerships, and equity dimensions where public sources permit.

gaps_acknowledged: No ECU chief AI officer or exact AI hub budget was found.
No dedicated public AI compute infrastructure was found.
Enrollment was left null rather than relying on non-source local data.
After targeted Worker 3 searches, ECU still did not disclose exact Copilot user counts, contract values, or a dedicated local AI GPU cluster.
ECU publishes broad student AI learning resources, but public sources did not disclose subgroup-level outcome metrics.
